---
# Body only — paper-level metadata lives in ../../_shared/papers.toml
bibliography:
  - ../../RTR-WP-006-city-libraries/paper/library-comparator-references.bib
  - ../../_shared/references.bib
format:
  pdf:
    output-file: "RTR-WP-007-body.pdf"
    documentclass: article
    classoption: [11pt, a4paper]
    geometry: [top=20mm, bottom=20mm, left=28mm, right=28mm]
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-depth: 2
    colorlinks: false
    linkcolor: "black"
    urlcolor: "black"
    citecolor: "black"
    include-in-header: ../../_shared/body-preamble.tex
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{tabularx}
      \usepackage{xurl}
      \usepackage{setspace}
      \onehalfspacing
      \usepackage{float}
      \floatplacement{figure}{!htbp}
      \floatplacement{table}{!htbp}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

India now has two public-facing digital library surfaces that carry the word
"national" in different registers. The National Digital Library of India
(NDLI), operated from IIT Kharagpur, presents itself as "One Library All of
India" and as a single-window educational repository. Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya,
under the Ministry of Education's school-education apparatus, presents itself
as a free digital reading library for children and youth.[^ndli-home]

These are not minor websites. They are state-backed answers to a major public
question: if India's physical public-library network is weak, can a national
digital service partially compensate? The answer depends on what "library"
means. If a library is only a pile of content with search over it, then a large
federated index might be enough. If a library is a public institution that
lowers the cost of reading, provides reader guidance, protects privacy, supports
many languages, enables accessible formats, and connects people to librarians
and local institutions, then the standard is higher.

This paper reviews NDLI and Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya against that higher
standard. The conclusion is deliberately two-sided. NDLI is not merely a garbage
dump: it has real taxonomy, domain facets, language affordances, content
provider logic, and institutional services. But its user experience often makes
that structure feel dump-like, because the interface asks readers to navigate
the system's categories before it answers their reading need. Rashtriya
e-Pustakalaya has the stronger public-library concept, because it starts from
children, stories, languages, and age bands. But its implementation weakens its
public character: the public About route is JavaScript-only, and the app source
blocks browser behaviors such as right-click and print shortcuts.

The core design claim is simple: India should stop trying to make one giant
portal stand for every library function. A credible public digital library
needs four connected layers: a reader library, a scholarly repository, a public
library network layer, and a preservation-and-rights layer.

[^ndli-home]: National Digital Library of India, `https://ndl.iitkgp.ac.in/`;
Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya About route, `https://ndl.education.gov.in/about`.
Both were fetched and reviewed on 25 June 2026. The Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya
route returned a JavaScript-only shell when fetched as HTML.

# Method and Review Standard

This is a heuristic UX review and service-model analysis, not a moderated
usability study. The evidence base is local-first: saved public HTML and source
artefacts for NDLI and Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya; the Right to Read public-library
measurement frame; local NFHS-5 digital-access figures; parliamentary text on
NDLI; and the partial-recall corpus for related library, access, and
Ambedkarite material. Official public pages for comparison models are used only
where the local corpus did not already contain a cleaner primary description of
the model.[^scope]

The review asks four questions:

1. Does the service help a reader get to a readable object?
2. Does it work under public conditions: weak device, weak network,
   non-English use, low institutional confidence, disability, and shared
   phones?
3. Does it behave like a library, meaning that it supports discovery, guidance,
   borrowing or downloading, rights clarity, accessibility, privacy, and human
   mediation?
4. Does it connect to the institutional life of libraries: local branches,
   teachers, librarians, preservation, and collection governance?

The target users are not a generic "digital user." A public digital library in
India must serve at least seven concrete publics:

- a child looking for a story in a home language;
- a school student looking for age-appropriate reading or textbooks;
- a teacher or anganwadi/library worker assembling a reading set;
- an exam aspirant looking for structured preparatory resources;
- a researcher needing discovery, citation, and metadata;
- a disabled or print-disabled reader needing audio, text-to-speech, EPUB,
  large type, or screen-reader compatibility;
- a librarian or institution needing hosting, preservation, collection
  management, and public-service tooling.

The review uses standard usability principles: visibility of system status,
match between system and user language, user control, consistency, error
prevention, recognition over recall, efficiency, minimalism, error recovery,
and help/documentation. But the public-library standard adds two further tests.
First, the service must reduce social barriers to reading. Second, it must not
turn copyright, analytics, account control, or app behavior into new gates.

[^scope]: The comparison models reviewed were DPLA, Europeana, Trove, the
National Library of Norway, the Palace Project, Libby, Open Library, Project
Gutenberg, StoryWeaver, and Bookshare.

# Public-Library and Ambedkarite Access Standard

The review standard is not "does the portal have a lot of content?" The
public-library standard is service performance. RTR-WP-006 uses the
IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto, IFLA's Library Map of the World, ISO
2789, and ISO 11620 as the measurement frame: a public library must be read as
a service system, with service points, staff, users, visits, loans, digital
loans, downloads, internet access, expenditure, and population reach, not as a
warehouse of books or files [@iflaManifesto2022; @iflaLibraryMapData; @iso2789;
@iso11620].

For a digital library in India, that means the digital layer cannot assume the
reader already has digital capacity. It must ask whether the service supplies,
bridges, or ignores the access conditions that a branch library would normally
mediate: a device, a place to sit, a readable language, privacy, a librarian or
teacher, print/offline fallback, assistive formats, and the confidence to use
the system without humiliation.

The Ambedkarite test is sharper. Ambedkar's critique of caste treats reading
and knowledge as historically withheld capacities, not merely as consumer
preferences. In *Annihilation of Caste*, he argues that caste destroys public
spirit and makes collective life impossible across graded boundaries (BAWS
Vol. 1, pp. 56-57). In the same text, he defines democracy socially: a mode of
associated living and shared experience, not only a constitutional form (BAWS
Vol. 1, pp. 57-58). In his account of the Shudras, the denial of reading and
writing appears as a structural political technology: literacy is reserved for a
few, while illiteracy is assigned to the many (BAWS Vol. 3, p. 43). A public
digital library therefore cannot be judged only by its collection size. It must
be judged by whether it breaks or reproduces that inherited distribution of
knowledge.

# Digital Substitution as Proceduralised Refusal

The digital-access evidence changes the politics of this review. The question is
not simply whether NDLI and Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya have usable interfaces. The
question is whether the State is using digital interfaces to substitute for the
library infrastructure it has not built. That substitution is a form of
proceduralised refusal: access is announced at the portal layer, while the
burden of device ownership, data, private screen time, English/interface
confidence, digital literacy, disability accommodation, and print/offline
fallback is pushed onto the reader.

The telecom story is real, but it is not the same as public access. TRAI's July
2025 subscription release recorded 1,220.02 million total telephone
subscriptions, 1,171.91 million wireless subscriptions, and 1,083.14 million
active wireless subscribers on peak VLR. The same release recorded 984.69
million broadband subscribers, but only 45.49 million fixed wired broadband
subscribers; mobile broadband was 930.41 million. Rural wireless mobile
tele-density was 58.20%, against 124.75% in urban India. The local December
2025 digital-access note records TRAI's later 1,028.6 million internet
subscriber figure, 1,007.4 million broadband subscribers, 47.4 million wireline
subscribers, 983.3 million wireless subscribers, and wireless ARPU of about
Rs. 195 per month.[^digital-access]

Those numbers are not a mandate to close libraries and open portals. They are a
warning about the kind of user a portal silently selects. A billion
subscriptions do not mean a billion autonomous readers. A SIM is not a person;
a monthly "active internet user" can be someone who used the internet once in a
month; a household phone is not a woman's phone; a mobile-data connection is
not a quiet reading room; a JavaScript app is not a library service.

| Measure | What the digital-success story says | What the library-access test asks |
|---|---|---|
| 1,083.14 million active wireless subscribers, July 2025 | India is connected | How many are individual readers with private, reliable access? |
| 984.69 million broadband subscribers, July 2025 | Broadband is near-universal | How much is fixed, stable, document-grade broadband? Only 45.49 million was fixed wired broadband. |
| 930.41 million mobile-broadband subscribers, July 2025 | Mobile solves access | Can a child, rural woman, disabled reader, or exam aspirant read, listen, print, cite, and return later on a shared phone? |
| 58.20% rural wireless mobile tele-density vs. 124.75% urban, July 2025 | Rural India is on the network | Rural access remains thinner, more shared, and more fragile. |
| 33.3% of women and 51.2% of men aged 15-49 ever used the internet, NFHS-5 India total | Internet adoption is rising | The public-library target population is precisely where autonomous use is lowest. |
| 20.6% of ST women and 27.0% of SC women ever used the internet, NFHS-5 | Digital can scale | Caste and gender compound; the portal default user is not the Bahujan woman reader. |
| 61% male vs. 31% female mobile-phone ownership, Oxfam India 2022 | Phones are widely available | Ownership, not household access, is the first condition of autonomous reading. |
| Women's mobile-internet adoption in India at 37%; rural women 45% less likely than rural men to use mobile internet | Smartphones will carry the public library | Mobile internet itself is gendered; a phone-first reader strategy is not gender-neutral [@gsma_gender_gap_2024]. |
| 75% upper-caste internet adoption vs. 38% Dalit and 28% Adivasi adoption; fixed broadband at home in 42% of upper-caste households, 8% of SC households, and 5% of ST households | "Online" reaches everyone equally | The home broadband reader is caste-stratified [@ceda_caste_digital_2025]. |
| About one-quarter of ST adults do not use a phone at all; nearly half use a shared phone | Mobile-first is enough | Shared-phone access cannot carry a rights-bearing public library [@sprf_st_digital_2022]. |
| Digital-literacy indicators are measured separately from simple connectivity in NSS survey instruments | Connectivity is capability | Digital literacy must be supplied or mediated by the service, not assumed as a precondition [@nsso_reading_2023]. |

![NFHS-5 internet use by caste and gender, from the local Right to Read figure set. The average line is 33.3% for women and 51.2% for men; Scheduled Tribe women are at 20.6%, Scheduled Caste women at 27.0%, OBC women at 33.3%, and "general" caste women at 44.2%.](figures/india-internet-caste-nfhs5.png){#fig-caste-internet width=100%}

This is where the UX review must stop being polite. A portal that assumes a
stable device, JavaScript, private screen time, print control, sufficient
bandwidth, a confident search vocabulary, and the ability to distinguish
interface language from content language is not serving India in general. It is
serving the already-addressable fraction of India. The "30-40%" intuition is
therefore directionally right if the question is meaningful, autonomous access
among the groups a public library must most care about: rural women, Dalit and
Adivasi readers, low-wealth households, first-generation learners, children
sharing family devices, and older or disabled readers who need mediation. It is
wrong only if telecom subscriptions are treated as the same thing as a usable
public reading environment.

The Grammar of Refusal essay gives the right language for this. A state answer
can refuse without saying "no" by moving the question into a register where the
named gap disappears. In library policy, "we have a national digital portal" can
perform that move. The child asks for a story in a language she can read; the
portal offers a JavaScript app. The teacher asks for printable reading packs;
the app blocks print shortcuts. The rural reader needs a place, device, and
librarian; the state counts wireless subscriptions. The Dalit or Adivasi woman
needs unmediated access; the metric counts household or SIM access. The
procedure is the refusal.

That does not make digital libraries useless. It defines their honest role.
They can serve connected students, researchers, teachers, librarians, exam
aspirants, and some children well. They can also support public institutions by
supplying offline packs, printable shelves, audio, EPUBs, metadata, and local
library workflows. But if they replace physical libraries, school libraries,
public internet points, and human mediation, they convert a public-library
promise into an elite access filter and call the excluded majority an adoption
problem.

[^digital-access]: TRAI July 2025 figures from *Highlights of Telecom
Subscription Data as on 31st July 2025*, Press Release No. 83/2025, 29 August
2025. December 2025 internet/broadband figures from the local digital-access
note, which records TRAI 2026 summary figures. IAMAI/Kantar 2026 defines
"active internet user" as at least one internet use in the past month. NFHS-5
gender and caste figures come from the local Right to Read NFHS-5 figure set,
which cites NFHS-5 India Report FR375, Table 3.6, p. 101. Mobile-phone
ownership figures are from Oxfam India 2022.

# NDLI IIT Kharagpur: Structured Repository, Weak Reader Path

The NDLI homepage is not an empty shell. It is server-rendered HTML with a
large navigational surface, a search box, language controls, institutional
contact information, and a set of domain cards. The public metadata describes
NDLI as a virtual repository of learning resources sponsored and mentored by
the Ministry of Education through NMEICT, developed and maintained from IIT
Kharagpur.[^ndli-source]

![Saved NDLI homepage HTML rendered from disk. The page contains real public information and language/search controls, but the local rendering also shows how asset dependence and layered controls can collapse into a cluttered, low-guidance entry surface.](figures/ndl-local-html.png){#fig-ndli-local width=100%}

NDLI's strengths are real. The homepage exposes domains for School Education,
Higher Education, Research, Career Development, Cultural Archives, Newspaper
Archives, Judicial Resources, Patents and Standards, and SWAYAM-linked skill
development. The search control includes Indian-language choices. The footer
and modal surfaces link to services that are recognizably library-facing:
Institutional Registration, NDLI Club, IDR Hosting Service, IDL Service, a
Digital Preservation Centre, a copyright guide for Indian libraries, feedback,
and contact routes.

That is not a random dump. It is a federated educational repository with
institutional services around it.

The problem is that the primary UX still behaves like a repository portal
rather than a public library. The first burden is classification. A user who
knows what they want should be able to type, search, filter, and read. On NDLI,
the domain structure is pushed to the front. The live homepage JavaScript
redirects search to `/_search?key=...`, but focusing the search field can also
trigger a domain-selector modal when the modal message is absent. That is a
classic flow break: a reader begins a search and the system asks them to choose
the institution's frame first.

The second burden is language. NDLI exposes multiple language mechanisms:
interface translation, search/input language, and content-language facets. Each
is useful. But they are not framed as distinct user tasks. For a Hindi, Bengali,
Tamil, Urdu, or Assamese reader, the question is practical: "Will I see the
site in my language? Can I type my query? Will I find content in that language?"
Those are three different controls. NDLI's current presentation risks making
the user infer the difference.

The third burden is the mismatch between slogan and service. "One Library All
of India" is an expansive public-library promise. But the observed service is
closer to a single discovery window over learning resources, with institutional
services attached. That can be valuable, especially to students, researchers,
and institutions. It is not the same as a public library.

Parliamentary material in the local corpus confirms the same institutional
identity. In 2018, the Ministry described NDLI as a collaborator with global
digital-library and heritage institutions: a 2017 workshop with UNESCO's
regional cluster office and representatives from 13 countries; an MoU with the
British Library for the Two Centuries of Indian Print Project; work with the
European digital-library foundation; RightsStatements.org membership; and
integration of sources such as OECD Library, PubMed, WHO, and publishers. That
is the profile of a federated repository and heritage/education infrastructure,
not a neighbourhood public-library substitute.

The public-library gap is visible in what the interface does not foreground. It
does not start from "I am in Class 8," "I need a Hindi story," "I have low
vision," "I need an audiobook," "I want something I can print," "I am a teacher
building a reading list," or "I am a district librarian setting up an offline
reading shelf." The service has categories, but not enough reader guidance.

[^ndli-source]: NDLI live homepage, `https://ndl.iitkgp.ac.in/`, fetched 25
June 2026.

## NDLI Heuristic Findings

**Critical / major issue: search flow interruption.** A search box should not
interrupt the user with a domain-choice modal at the moment of focus. Domain
narrowing belongs after the query or alongside results.

**Major issue: system model over user model.** The IA is organized around
domains and resource classes. The main missing layer is task-based access:
children, teachers, exam aspirants, researchers, disabled readers, librarians.

**Major issue: language control ambiguity.** Interface language, search input
language, and content language should be separated and labelled as such.

**Major issue: insufficient public-library mediation.** NDLI has institutional
services but does not strongly expose human guidance, local-library connection,
reader advisory, accessible reading paths, or offline packs.

**Strength to preserve.** The domain taxonomy, institutional service links,
language affordances, preservation/hosting services, and feedback taxonomy are
assets. The redesign should not flatten NDLI into a generic search page. It
should put reader pathways over the existing structure.

# Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya: Better Library Concept, Worse Public-Web Discipline

Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya is conceptually sharper than NDLI. Its About component
states that it was launched on 10 February 2024 by the Minister of Education
Dharmendra Pradhan, sits under the Department of School Education and Literacy,
and is designed for children and youth. The same component claims over 6500
non-academic titles, more than 250 publishers, 23+ Indian languages and English,
and formats including PDF, EPUB, text-to-speech, and audiobooks.[^rep-source]

Those are the right nouns for a public reading library. Children, youth,
non-academic books, languages, age bands, stories, PDF, EPUB, audio, and
text-to-speech map onto real library needs far better than a generic national
knowledge repository does. Its app source also shows a home-page model organized
around popular books, books by language, newly added books, video books, books
by age group, nursery rhymes, genre, authors, publishers, and recommendations.

But the implementation contradicts the mission.

The About route is JavaScript-only. Fetched as HTML, it returns the message
"You need to enable JavaScript to run this app" and does not expose the About
content. That is unacceptable for a public library. A public library's public
pages should be readable by crawlers, assistive technology, locked-down school
machines, low-end devices, and users on bad networks. JavaScript can enhance
the experience. It cannot be the condition for basic public information.

![Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya's saved About route rendered from the public HTML with JavaScript disabled. The only visible fallback is "You need to enable JavaScript to run this app."](figures/rep-about-nojs-local-html.png){#fig-rep-nojs width=100%}

The second problem is more serious: the app source disables the browser context
menu, blocks `Ctrl+P`, and uses a devtools-detection redirect to a warning page.
This is a hostile pattern in a learning environment. A public library should not
treat printing, quoting, saving, inspecting, or using browser tools as suspect
behavior. If there are rights constraints, the service should express them
through licensing, download rules, watermarking where appropriate, and clear
terms. It should not break ordinary browser affordances.

Blocking print is especially damaging. Indian reading contexts are hybrid:
children share phones, teachers print pages, parents save stories for offline
use, disabled readers use assistive workflows, and low-bandwidth users need
fallbacks. A print block says that the service is optimized around app control,
not reading.

[^rep-source]: Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya public About route,
`https://ndl.education.gov.in/about`, plus the public JavaScript bundle and
source map linked by that route, fetched 25 June 2026. Numeric collection claims
are treated here as site claims, not independently audited catalog totals.

## Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya Heuristic Findings

**Critical issue: no-JS public-page failure.** Public informational pages should
render meaningful content without JavaScript.

**Critical issue: anti-reader browser controls.** Disabling right-click,
printing, and devtools conflicts with the public-library mission.

**Major issue: app-first access model.** The service may work well as an app
for users with capable devices, but public-library access must include web,
offline, low-bandwidth, print, and assistive modes.

**Major issue: engagement may displace service.** Routes for rewards,
competitions, reviews, recommendations, and profiles may be useful, but they
should not crowd out the basic library tasks: find, read, download, print,
listen, cite, share with a class, and return later.

**Strength to preserve.** The children/youth focus, age-band organization,
language shelves, genre shelves, and multi-format ambition are exactly the
right foundation for a national reader library.

# The Combined Problem: India Has Portals, Not a Library Service Stack

Read together, NDLI and Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya show a split. NDLI has the
repository and institutional side. Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya has the reader side.
Neither fully solves the public-library problem because neither is embedded
enough in a national public-library service stack.

A library stack is not a website. It is a set of institutional promises:

- discovery: users can find what exists;
- access: users can open, borrow, download, print, listen, or request;
- guidance: users receive age, level, subject, language, and context help;
- rights clarity: users know what they may do with the object;
- accessibility: users can read in formats that work for them;
- preservation: the record and object remain stable over time;
- local mediation: teachers, librarians, schools, and local branches can use
  the system with communities;
- accountability: the public can see coverage, gaps, usage, licensing, and
  collection policy.

NDLI performs parts of discovery, preservation, and institutional connection.
Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya performs parts of reader access and child-facing
discovery. The missing middle is public-library mediation: the connection to
India's district libraries, municipal libraries, school libraries, teachers,
anganwadi systems, disability services, and community reading programs.

The design implication is that "national digital library" should not be treated
as a single surface. It should be treated as connected public infrastructure.

The Ambedkarite conclusion is that "portal first" is not a neutral technical
choice. It chooses the already-addressable user as the default public and turns
the Bahujan, rural, disabled, poor, and phone-sharing reader into an exception
to be handled later. That is the governing-class UX pattern: service design
starts from the administratively legible, device-owning user, then calls the
excluded majority an adoption problem.

The NDLI/ReP split is therefore not merely a product-management problem. NDLI
refuses the reader's register by asking a readerly question to pass first
through repository categories. ReP refuses public-web access by making even its
About route a JavaScript app shell and by blocking ordinary reader actions such
as printing. In both cases the institution can truthfully say that a digital
library exists. What it cannot honestly say is that the design has carried the
reader across the access gap. The gap is being administratively relocated from
the state to the user.

# Public Digital Library Models Worth Studying

The comparison set below is not a ranking. The models have different legal and
institutional mandates. They are useful because each solves one part of the
library problem better than the Indian services currently do.

## Trove: Discovery Plus Physical Library Connection

Trove, maintained by the National Library of Australia, describes itself as a
free, always-online service for searching collections from Australian
libraries, universities, museums, galleries, and archives.[^trove] It brings
together digital copies and physical holdings. Crucially, it also points users
toward options for viewing physical items, including library loans. Trove also
foregrounds community contribution through Voluntroves, API and bulk-download
options, help for downloading, printing, citing, and culturally specific
guidance around First Australians content.

The lesson for India is that national discovery should not float above the
library system. It should connect digital objects, physical holdings, local
service, community correction, and reuse.

[^trove]: Trove About, National Library of Australia,
`https://trove.nla.gov.au/about`.

## Europeana: Aggregation as Public Data Infrastructure

Europeana gives access to Europe's digital cultural heritage through a network
of aggregators and providing institutions. Its public About page lists item
counts by media type and says it displays digital cultural heritage from over
2,000 European providing institutions. Europeana Pro describes the wider
infrastructure: data providers, aggregators, APIs, the common European data
space for cultural heritage, reuse, accessibility, and metadata quality.[^europeana]

The lesson for India is that aggregation is a discipline. It requires metadata
standards, rights statements, provider accountability, APIs, reuse policy, and
capacity-building for institutions. Without that discipline, aggregation becomes
a dump.

[^europeana]: Europeana About, `https://www.europeana.eu/en/about-us`;
Europeana Pro mission, `https://pro.europeana.eu/about-us/mission`.

## DPLA and Palace: Library Control Over Digital Lending

The Digital Public Library of America describes two linked functions: making
millions of materials from libraries, archives, museums, and cultural
institutions available in a one-stop discovery experience, and providing a
library-controlled marketplace/platform for ebooks and other e-content.[^dpla]
The Palace Project, supported by Lyrasis in strategic partnership with DPLA,
frames its goal as strengthening the public library as the digital center of
the community, giving libraries more direct patron relationships, greater
control over e-content acquisition and delivery, publisher advocacy, and patron
privacy.[^palace]

The lesson for India is governance. Digital reading should not be reduced to a
central app. Public libraries need institutional control over collections,
patron relationships, privacy rules, and community service.

[^dpla]: DPLA About, `https://dp.la/about`.

[^palace]: The Palace Project About, `https://thepalaceproject.org/about/`.

## Libby: Excellent Reader UX, Proprietary Governance

Libby shows what a polished public-library reading app can feel like. It links
borrowing to a library card, supports ebooks, audiobooks, magazines, offline
downloads or streaming, holds notifications, tags, font/layout/lighting
customization, notes, highlights, and audiobook controls.[^libby]

The governance model is proprietary and should not be copied uncritically. But
the UX patterns matter. A reader library must make the first task easy: find
your library, sign in if needed, borrow or open, download for offline use, and
read or listen in a comfortable format.

[^libby]: Libby / OverDrive app page,
`https://www.overdrive.com/apps/libby`.

## National Library of Norway: Rights-Aware National Digitization

The National Library of Norway states that it is digitizing its entire
collection; the digitization programme began in 2006 and was estimated to take
20-30 years. Its digital access page says the digital library contains most
books published in Norway, plus newspapers, periodicals, manuscripts, recorded
music, and other cultural-heritage objects. Access depends on copyright,
location, and purpose: public-domain material can be available to everyone,
agreement-covered copyrighted material can be available under conditions such
as Norwegian IP access, and the full digital collection can be available on
premises for research/documentation.[^norway]

The lesson for India is legal clarity. A serious national digital library needs
legal deposit, digitization policy, rights agreements, on-premise access rules,
research access, public-domain downloads, and stable identifiers. "Everything
online" is not a policy. A rights-aware access model is.

[^norway]: National Library of Norway, "Digitizing at the National Library,"
`https://www.nb.no/en/digitizing-at-the-national-library/`; "Access to the
National Library's digital library," `https://www.nb.no/en/digital-access/`.

## Open Library and Project Gutenberg: Open Records and Low-Friction Text

Open Library's goal is one web page for every book ever published. It states
that it has gathered over 20 million records and that its software, data, and
documentation are open.[^openlib] Project Gutenberg is narrower and simpler:
more than 75,000 free ebooks, free and always free.[^gutenberg]

The lesson for India is that some layers should be radically simple. Public
domain Indian-language texts, open government publications, children's books
with open licenses, and basic bibliographic records should not require heavy
apps, accounts, or portal navigation.

[^openlib]: Open Library About, `https://openlibrary.org/about`.

[^gutenberg]: Project Gutenberg About, `https://www.gutenberg.org/about/`.

## StoryWeaver: A Strong Indian Reference Model for Open Children's Reading

StoryWeaver from Pratham Books is a useful Indian comparator, but it should not
be overstated as a complete public-library model. Its own About page describes
it as a digital gateway to thousands of richly illustrated, open-licensed
children's stories in mother-tongue languages. It says users can read, create,
translate, and download stories, and that stories are available as
web-resolution PDF, A4 print-ready PDF, and EPUB. Its open-content page states
that StoryWeaver stories and illustrations use the CC BY 4.0 license. Its FAQ
adds the important operational qualification: StoryWeaver is not itself usable
without internet access, but users can download stories while online and then
read them later or print them.[^storyweaver]

The lesson for Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya is therefore narrower than "copy
StoryWeaver." At the children's-reading layer, StoryWeaver is a strong Indian
reference for open licensing, multilingual story discovery, translation and
re-levelling workflows, printable PDFs, EPUB, and downloaded/offline use of
individual stories. It does not solve the whole public-library problem:
institutional distribution, disability-first formats, privacy, collection
governance, and integration with public libraries and schools still need a
separate public-service design.

[^storyweaver]: Pratham Books StoryWeaver About,
`https://storyweaver.org.in/en/about`; StoryWeaver Open Content,
`https://storyweaver.org.in/en/open-content`; StoryWeaver FAQs,
`https://storyweaver.org.in/en/faqs`.

## Bookshare: Accessibility as Collection Infrastructure

Bookshare describes itself as an accessible ebook library for people who cannot
read traditional books. It reports over 1.4 million titles, accessible formats,
over 40 languages, and partnerships with over 1,000 publishers.[^bookshare]

The lesson for India is that accessibility cannot be an afterthought. If a
digital library claims text-to-speech or support for disabled readers, it needs
an actual accessible-collection policy: EPUB quality, screen-reader testing,
DAISY or equivalent navigation where needed, alt text, structured text, audio
quality, and a clear route for print-disabled users.

[^bookshare]: Bookshare "Who we are," `https://www.bookshare.org/who-we-are`.

# Design Direction for India

The worst design move would be to merge everything into a larger portal. India
should instead define four layers and connect them through shared identity,
metadata, rights, and local-library interfaces.

## Layer 1: Reader Library

This is the public-facing reading service. Its primary users are children,
parents, teachers, new readers, and general public readers.

Minimum standard:

- works without JavaScript for public pages;
- low-bandwidth mode;
- no login for open/free/public-domain material;
- clear age bands and reading levels;
- content language, interface language, and input language separated;
- PDF, EPUB, audio, and TTS where rights allow;
- print and offline download supported rather than blocked;
- curated shelves by age, language, theme, school use, and reading level;
- accessible reader controls: font size, spacing, contrast, audio speed, notes,
  bookmarks, and resume.

Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya should own this layer, but it needs public-web
discipline and should borrow specific patterns from StoryWeaver's open-license,
translation, printable, and downloaded-reading model, and from the reader UX of
Libby.

## Layer 2: Scholarly Repository

This is the search and research layer. Its primary users are higher-education
students, researchers, exam aspirants, teachers, and institutions.

Minimum standard:

- powerful search and filters;
- stable item pages;
- citation export;
- metadata provenance;
- content provider identity;
- subject and discipline facets;
- full-text search where possible;
- clear rights and access conditions;
- APIs for institutional reuse.

NDLI is closest to this layer. It should stop carrying the entire "one library"
promise and instead become excellent at repository discovery, academic search,
and institutional integration.

## Layer 3: Public Library Network Layer

This is the missing layer. It connects digital content to actual public
institutions.

Minimum standard:

- every district and municipal library has a public profile;
- branch hours, services, internet availability, accessibility, staff, events,
  and holdings are visible;
- librarians can create local shelves and QR reading lists;
- teachers can create class reading packs;
- district systems can download offline packs;
- users can find nearby physical libraries and know what they can do there;
- public usage data is published in a standard format.

This is where India's current digital library strategy is weakest. Without it,
the national platform becomes a content surface above a broken local network.

## Layer 4: Preservation and Rights Layer

This is the national-memory layer.

Minimum standard:

- legal-deposit strategy for digital publications;
- digitization standards;
- persistent identifiers;
- public-domain download policy;
- rights statements on every item;
- research/on-premise access for restricted works;
- institutional hosting and preservation services;
- metadata exports and APIs;
- audit trails for takedowns, corrections, and rights changes.

NDLI's IDR, IDL, and Digital Preservation Centre point in this direction, but
the layer should be explicit and accountable.

# Product Recommendations

The first 90 days should be surgical.

1. Remove ReP's right-click, print-shortcut, and devtools blocks.
2. Server-render ReP's About, FAQ, guide, catalog landing, and collection-policy
   pages.
3. Separate language controls across both services: interface language, content
   language, and input/script helper.
4. Stop interrupting NDLI search focus with a domain-selector modal.
5. Add task-based entry points to NDLI: school student, child reader, teacher,
   researcher, exam aspirant, librarian, accessible reader.
6. Publish a rights label on every readable item: read online, download,
   print, share, classroom use, public domain, open license, restricted.
7. Add low-bandwidth and print/offline flows to the reader library.
8. Publish accessibility statements and test results for both services.
9. Create librarian and teacher toolkits: printable reading lists, QR shelves,
   offline packs, local-language campaign templates.
10. Publish service metrics: collection by language, age band, format, rights
    status, accessibility status, and usage.
11. Publish digital-exclusion metrics beside usage metrics: no-login items,
    public-domain downloads, printable items, audio/EPUB coverage, low-bandwidth
    page weight, public-library access points, and use through schools or
    libraries rather than only individual accounts.
12. Publish an access-prerequisites statement for every major workflow: whether
    it works without JavaScript, whether it works on low-end phones, whether it
    works on a shared device without exposing reading history, approximate page
    weight/data cost, printability, downloadability, and whether a librarian or
    teacher can mediate it for a reader with low digital literacy.
13. Report equity of use rather than only volume of use: language, rural/urban
    geography, school/library mediated use, disability format, and outreach
    through SC/ST/OBC, minority, and girls' education institutions where data
    collection can be done without surveillance or stigma.
14. Treat branch libraries, school libraries, panchayat reading rooms, and
    disability services as distribution infrastructure, not as "outreach" after
    the portal is built.

The next 12 months should define the institutional stack:

- NDLI as scholarly repository and institutional services layer;
- Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya as national reader library;
- a new public-library network layer tied to state, district, municipal, and
  school libraries;
- a preservation/rights layer with legal-deposit and digitization policy.

# Conclusion

The question is not whether NDLI or Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya contains useful
content. They do. The question is whether they serve library needs.

NDLI serves some library needs of students, researchers, and institutions. But
it is not yet a public library in the civic sense. Its strength is structured
aggregation; its weakness is that it makes readers meet the repository halfway.

Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya is closer to the public-reading mission, especially for
children and Indian-language reading. But a public library cannot be a
JavaScript-only app shell that blocks print. Its mission and implementation are
currently at odds.

A library is not a content dump. It is a public arrangement that makes reading
possible for people who would otherwise be excluded from it. India's digital
library strategy should be judged by that standard. On that standard, NDLI is a
real repository but not yet a public library; Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya has a
stronger reader mission but currently violates public-web discipline; and the
combined state strategy serves connected Indians first unless it is anchored in
libraries, schools, offline access, accessible formats, and the social question
Ambedkar forces us to ask: who is being made capable of reading?

# Data and Code Availability {.unnumbered}

This paper is a heuristic UX review; its evidence base is the saved public HTML and source artefacts of the National Digital Library of India and Rashtriya e-Pustakalaya, NFHS-5 digital-access figures, and the parliamentary record on NDLI. The review rubric and the per-portal evaluation are deposited as the derived artifacts in the Right to Read working-paper series' Open Science Framework (OSF) project, with any analysis code archived for a citable, versioned DOI on Zenodo (DOIs to be assigned at public release). The reviewed portals and primary documents are cited to their own locations rather than re-hosted here. The deposited derived material is released under CC-BY-4.0 and any analysis code under the GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPL-3.0).
